plant.description: Unique History: Named after the legendary Sage Agastya, who is considered one of the 'Saptarishis' and the father of traditional Siddha medicine. The plant is often planted near temples and is considered 'Sattvic' in nature. It is mentioned in all major Brihat-trayi texts for its unique ability to clear the 'head' (Shira). Physical Characteristics: A fast-growing, slender, softwood tree reaching up to 10-12 meters. It features pinnate leaves and large, showy, pea-like flowers (white or red). The fruit is a long, thin, hanging pod reaching up to 30-50 cm. It is widely distributed across the tropical regions of India, Southeast Asia, and Northern Australia. Specific Chemical Compounds: Rich in Sesbanimide (anti-tumor property research), Grandiflorol, Saponins, and Tannins. The presence of high concentrations of Vitamin A, Iron, and Calcium in the leaves makes it a nutritional powerhouse in various ethnic cuisines. Strict Standardizations: API standards specify macroscopic identification of the bark (greyish-brown, fissured) and leaves. Physicochemical parameters include total ash (<10%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%), and water-soluble extractive (>15%). HPTLC profiles based on oleanolic acid are used for quality assurance.

rasa_taste: Tikta (bitter), Kashaya (astringent). The flowers specifically have a Madhura (sweet) secondary taste.
ayurvedic_properties.virya_potency: Sheeta (cooling - specifically for flowers and leaves), though the root bark is considered slightly Ushna (heating) in some clinical contexts.
ayurvedic_properties.dosha_karma: Primarily pacifies Pitta and Kapha doshas. While the flowers are notably Sheeta (cooling) and pacify Pitta, the root and bark have a slight Ushna (heating) tendency that helps in clearing Kapha-related respiratory blocks. It is balanced and does not significantly aggravate Vata when used appropriately.

nasal_drops__nasya_.method: 2-4 drops of fresh leaf juice are instilled in each nostril for sinusitis and morning headaches.

decoction__kwatha_.method: 20g of bark boiled in 160ml water, reduced to 40ml. Filter and consume twice daily for fever and respiratory congestion.

curry_flower_preparation.method: Flowers are cleaned (stamina removed), chopped, and lightly sautéed in ghee with a pinch of black pepper for night blindness.

root_powder.method: 1-3g of dried root powder mixed with honey for chronic cough and localized swelling.

bark_paste.method: External application of bark grounded with water for skin inflammations and localized pain.

dosage.children: Leaf juice: 2-5 ml mixed with equal part honey. Bark decoction: 5-10 ml, only under expert supervision.
dosage.adults: Leaf juice: 10-20 ml. Bark decoction: 30-50 ml. Powder: 3-6 grams divided throughout the day. Arishta: 15-20 ml with equal water.
dosage.elderly: Preferably used in the form of warm decoctions (20-30ml) to ensure easy absorption and minimal digestive strain.

medicinal_uses: Respiratory and Sinus Health: The leaf juice is famously used as Nasya (nasal drops) to treat chronic sinusitis and 'head fullness' associated with Kapha. Its antimicrobial properties help clear secondary infections in the respiratory tract.

Vision and Eye Care: Rich in Vitamin A precursors, the flowers and leaves are traditionally indicated for Naktyandhya (night blindness). Consumption of flowers cooked in ghee is a classical remedy for improving optical health.

Antipyretic Activity: Acts as a potent Jwarahara (fever reducer), particularly useful in intermittent fevers. It helps modulate body temperature by clearing Srotas (channels) and promoting healthy perspiration.

Hepatoprotective Potential: Aligned with WHO botanical research, Agastya exhibits significant liver-protective properties. It helps in detoxifying the blood and supports healthy bile secretion, aiding in the management of jaundice symptoms.

Anti-inflammatory and Antimicrobial: The bark decoction is used to treat localized inflammation and as a wash for chronic ulcers. It contains tannins and flavonoids that act as natural astringents to promote wound healing.
Digestive Tonic: Stimulates the Agni (digestive fire) without causing excessive heat, making it beneficial for those with sensitive Pitta-Kapha constitutions suffering from low appetite.

Phytochemical Profile: Contains oleanolic acid, cystine, isoleucine, asparagine, phenylalanine, and various saponins. The red-flowered variety is particularly rich in anthocyanins and specific flavonoids.

safety_profile.warning: GRAS Status: Leaves and flowers are traditionally consumed as food (vegetable) in India and Thailand, confirming its 'Generally Recognized As Safe' status at dietary and therapeutic levels.
safety_profile.medical_supervision: Use under the guidance of a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner.
contraindications: Generally very safe. However, excessive consumption of the seeds can cause diarrhea due to high fiber and saponin content. Avoid during acute diarrhea or dysentery unless specifically prescribed.
possible_side_effects: Overdose of bark decoction may cause mild nausea or abdominal discomfort in very sensitive individuals.
drug_interactions: Monitor if taking with anti-diabetic medications as it may have a mild blood-sugar-lowering effect. No major toxic interactions recorded in WHO monographs.

plant_profile.note: AI estimated profile based on known properties
